questions.prompt: What is a warp, and why does it matter?

choices.text: A bug in the GPU driver.

A warp is a group of 32 threads that execute the same instruction at the same time on different data — NVIDIA's hardware scheduling unit. The GPU has one instruction fetcher per 32 threads rather than per individual thread, which is what makes thousands of threads economically feasible in silicon. It matters because every GPU performance pattern flows from this: block sizes should be multiples of 32; branches that diverge within a warp force the hardware to serialize both paths (halving or worse throughput); memory accesses are coalesced into 128-byte transactions matching a warp's natural width; and occupancy is measured in warps-per-SM, not threads. Understanding warps is the difference between writing GPU code that gets near-peak performance and code that gets 10% of peak.

questions.explanation: Warps are the unit of hardware scheduling. Almost every CUDA performance rule traces back to warp behavior. Knowing this prevents the most common GPU performance bugs.

questions.prompt: A CUDA kernel has this pattern: `if (data[idx] > threshold) heavy_path(); else light_path();`. Performance is much worse than expected. What's happening?

choices.text: Branch divergence within warps. When some threads in a warp take the `heavy_path` branch and others take the `light_path` branch, the SIMT hardware cannot execute both paths simultaneously because all 32 threads share one instruction fetcher. The hardware serializes: first the whole warp executes `heavy_path` with the 'light' threads masked off, then the whole warp executes `light_path` with the 'heavy' threads masked off. Total cost is the sum of both paths, not the average. The fix depends on the workload: sort/group data so threads in the same warp take the same branch; refactor so both branches do similar work; or split the kernel into two separate kernels (one for items above threshold, one for below). Heavily divergent code can run 5-10x slower than convergent code — this is the most common SIMT performance trap.

questions.explanation: Branch divergence is the signature SIMT performance bug. The fix is almost never 'more threads' — it's restructuring the work so threads in a warp do the same thing. This is why GPU-friendly algorithms often look different from CPU-friendly versions of the same problem.
